fingerprint: NP.xy pole positions are outputs of IGRF/gufm spherical harmonic models — globe-derived model outputs treated as raw observations
globe_value: The NOAA NP.xy dataset is not a direct measurement — it is the computed output of the International Geomagnetic Reference Field (IGRF) model, which solves Laplace's equation in spherical coordinates using spherical harmonics (associated Legendre polynomials). For pre-1900 data, the gufm1 historical model (Jackson et al., 2000) reconstructs pole positions from ship declination logs and observatory records, all processed through spherical harmonic inversion assuming a spherical Earth. The 'North Magnetic Pole' is defined as the point where field inclination = 90° (the dip pole), computed from the global spherical model — not measured directly by placing a compass at that exact location.
dome_value: The dome model treats NP.xy coordinates as raw observational data: 'NOAA NP.xy dataset confirms exactly this. Pre-1990: 0.0466 deg/year linear drift from 1590-1990.' No acknowledgment that the 1590-1990 trajectory is itself a model output (gufm1 → IGRF), not a chain of direct positional measurements.
match_precision: N/A — this is borrowed infrastructure, not a numerical constant match
why_it_matters: The dome model's WIN-006 presents the NP trajectory as empirical data that confirms dome physics. But the trajectory itself is computed from a spherical Earth model. The magnetic field is decomposed into spherical harmonics Y_l^m(θ,φ) — Legendre polynomials on a sphere — and the 'pole position' is derived from this decomposition. On a flat disc, the field would be decomposed into cylindrical harmonics (Bessel functions J_m), not spherical harmonics, and the resulting 'pole position' would differ. The dome model cannot use IGRF-derived pole positions as evidence for dome physics without first showing that a flat-disc magnetic field model produces the same trajectory — something it has never done and cannot do, because it has no magnetic field dynamics model at all (just 'aetheric vortex dynamics' with no equations).
additional_notes: This fingerprint is moderate in strength. Near the geographic North Pole, the azimuthal equidistant projection preserves distances and angles well, so the numerical pole coordinates would be similar in both projections. The fingerprint is strongest for the pre-1900 data (1590-1830) where the reconstruction depends entirely on the gufm1 spherical harmonic model — there were no direct pole measurements before the 19th century. It is also relevant to the 0.0466 deg/yr rate: 'degrees' here are angular displacement on a sphere (great-circle arc), not planar displacement on a disc, though the difference is small at high latitudes. The deeper issue is conceptual: the dome treats a globe model's output as raw data, which is a pattern we see across many WINs (NOAA data products are processed through globe-assuming frameworks).
suggested_text: The NOAA NP.xy dataset that WIN-006 cites as observational evidence is not raw measurement data — it is the output of the International Geomagnetic Reference Field (IGRF) model, which decomposes the Earth's magnetic field into spherical harmonics (Legendre polynomials in spherical coordinates). For the pre-1900 portion of the 1590–1990 trajectory, the pole positions are reconstructed entirely from the gufm1 historical field model (Jackson et al., 2000), which inverts ship declination logs through spherical harmonic analysis. The dome model treats this globe-model output as confirming flat-disc physics, without acknowledging that the trajectory was computed assuming a spherical Earth.
